body.en:
## The cast

Everything here lives on the following items, and yes, I named pretty much everything related to Stellaris, one of my favorite RTS games.


| Codename | Hardware | Role |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **citadel-core** | TP-Link AX1500 | The router, the front gate of the whole thing. |
| **trade-hub** | TP-Link TL-SG108E | A simple 8 port switch to handle VLANs for cybersec forensic stuff. |
| **sentry-array** | Raspberry Pi 5 | Pi-Hole: DNS + network-wide ad-blocking. The watcher. |
| **curator-node** | The server (not with the best specs 'cause I recycled a lot of old hardware I had) | Docker host, nginx, local LLMs, the workhorse that does the actual *work* |
| **juggernaut** | My desktop and daily driver | Personal and main workstation, plus several developer and cybersec tools I use on a daily basis |


(There's also a NAS which I'll code name as the `grand-archive`, it's in place for the future, to 'de-cloud,' stop paying Google Drive a cent more, and run my own storage myself.

The important one is **curator-node**. Almost everything I'm about to show you runs there.

## How anything finds anything

Here's the trick that makes the whole thing feel less like a server closet and more like a tiny private internet: I don't type IP addresses. Anyone on my network, my laptop, my phone, my girlfriend's Windows machine, everyone can just type `vw.curator-node.lan` and land on VaultWarden. No ports, no memorizing octets.

That magic is done by **Pi-Hole** with its local DNS records config, running on the **sentry-array**. The router (citadel-core) hands out the Pi-Hole as the network's DNS server over DHCP, so *every* device on the LAN asks it to resolve names. Pi-Hole holds the local records — `*.curator-node.lan` all point at the server — and as a bonus it nukes ads and trackers for the entire house while it's at it.

So when you ask for `vw.curator-node.lan`, sentry-array quietly answers "*that's curator-node*," and your browser heads there without ever knowing it did any work. (Worth saying plainly: Pi-Hole only answers the *name* question — it hands your device an IP and steps out of the way. It never touches the actual traffic.)

![pi-hole-local-dns-config](/images/writing/pi-hole-local-dns-config.png)

## One door, many rooms

Once a request reaches curator-node, **nginx** is the bouncer. Instead of a chaotic pile of `ip:port` combos, nginx listens on `80`/`443` and routes purely by *hostname* — each `*.curator-node.lan` name maps to whatever container or service is hiding behind it.

Here's the whole path in one shot, name resolution on the left (Pi-Hole), routing and the service fan-out on the right (nginx):

![writing/home-lab-topology](/images/writing/home-lab-topology.png)

There's even an old Apache + PHP page sitting at the bare `curator-node.lan` that's *supposed* to document all of this. It is perpetually out of date. Honestly, it being out of date is half the reason this post exists.

## The "Not Secure" confession

Okay, the embarrassing part. Look at that diagram again and you'll notice most of it is plain HTTP. Here's the honest story:

I use **mkcert** to spin up a local certificate authority, which gives real, trusted-looking TLS to internal services. The catch nobody mentions: that CA is only trusted on devices and **browsers where you actually imported it**. I imported it into Brave. So VaultWarden and Portainer look perfectly green and secure… *in Brave*. Open the same URL in Firefox or on my phone and it screams **NOT SECURE** like I'm running a phishing site.

Pi-Hole's admin panel does its own HTTPS thing (also untrusted). GitLab, NetData, OpenWebUI, Syncthing and the rest? Straight HTTP, no cert at all.

Do I lose sleep over it? Nope, *none of this touches the public internet* (more on that below). But "set up a proper internal CA and import it everywhere" is sitting far from the top of the to-do list, waiting for me to come some day and fix all these SSL problems.

## What's actually running

The reason the lab exists, grouped by what it's for:

- **VaultWarden**: Self-hosted password manager (a Bitwarden-compatible server). This is the one piece I genuinely *trust my life to*, so it gets the real cert and the careful treatment, I stopped paying for 1Password a while ago and I use this one instead, when I'm not at home I'll try to manage my passwords in the best way possible and then update them at home with new or modified entries.
- **Portainer**: The control panel for all my Docker containers, across *both* curator-node and juggernaut, from one place. Lazy in the best way.
- **GitLab CE**: My own private Git host with CI. Because not everything needs to live on someone else's servers.
- **OpenWebUI + Ollama**: Local LLMs running on the RTX 2060 right there in curator-node. Private, offline, no API bill, no telemetry. A small but very satisfying flex.
- **NetData**: Real-time monitoring, so when something falls over I find out before the family does.
- **Syncthing**: Peer-to-peer file sync without a cloud middleman, very useful to be honest to use with my laptop, I usually sync files like `.zshrc` to keep in sync my terminal preferences.
- **Postgres + PostGIS**: The databases quietly backing the projects I build. Not web-facing, just doing the work.
- **OWASP Juice Shop + WebGoat**: Deliberately broken apps. My personal, *legal*, throwaway pentesting range. These only spin up when I'm actively poking at them.

## On the chopping block

Being honest about the cruft, because every homelab has some: **n8n** (set it up, never built the automations I imagined) and **SonarQube** (currently disabled, eating resources for nothing). Both are getting evicted next time I'm in a cleaning mood.

## Why it's all LAN-only (and why that matters)

Not one of these services is reachable from the public internet. Those `.lan` names resolve to *nothing* outside my house — which is exactly why I'm comfortable showing you the whole map. The day I expose any of this through a tunnel or a DMZ, half this post becomes a free recon sheet for the wrong people, and I'll be sanitizing it *fast*.

That's also the genuine advice buried in here: a homelab is the best place on earth to learn this stuff precisely *because* you can keep it walled off while you make every mistake in the book. Make them in here, where the only person who can get owned is you.

## What's next

A proper internal CA so I stop seeing red in Firefox. Real backups for VaultWarden specifically (a password manager with no backup is a beautifully organized way to lose everything). Maybe — *maybe* — exposing one or two carefully chosen services to the outside world for my preference, as VaultWarden and the Storage Cloud with the NAS I'll buy, the right way.

But that's a different post. And knowing me, the Apache topology page still won't be updated by then.
